{"step_by_step_thinking": "The researcher isolates CD8-positive cells, which are cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs). When mixed with infected cells and crosslinked, immunoprecipitation of CD8 reveals a protein composed of two chains from an adjacent cell. This indicates a protein complex formed between the CD8 molecule on one cell and a molecule on the adjacent infected cell.

CD8 acts as a co-receptor for MHC class I molecules on the surface of infected cells. MHC class I molecules present peptides derived from intracellular proteins, including viral proteins in the case of HIV infection. These peptides are generated by proteasomal degradation of cytosolic proteins and transported into the endoplasmic reticulum via the TAP (transporter associated with antigen processing) channel.

The peptide is loaded onto MHC class I in the ER, and the MHC class I-peptide complex is transported to the cell surface. The CD8 molecule binds to the MHC class I molecule, stabilizing the interaction and facilitating recognition by the T cell.

The protein bound to CD8 that is composed of two chains from an adjacent cell is therefore the MHC class I molecule, which is composed of a heavy chain (alpha chain) and a beta-2-microglobulin chain. The alpha chain is synthesized in the ER and is responsible for binding the peptide, while beta-2-microglobulin stabilizes the complex.

The peptide presented by MHC class I is derived from endogenous proteins (such as viral proteins) and is transported via the TAP channel. Therefore, the protein bound to CD8 is MHC class I, which primarily binds endogenous peptides that have been transported by the TAP channel.

Option C correctly describes this function.", "answer_choice": "C"}
